rCoding.summary: test sizing of patterns array with eight short entries

patterns.pattern: Collapsed-by-default tool-call rows (verb, target, result summary) that expand on click
patterns.products: Claude Code, Cursor, Zed Agent Panel, GitHub Copilot Chat, Cline/Roo
patterns.why: dozens of calls per run; collapsed rows keep a scannable narrative, detail one click away
patterns.applicability: render each swarmx worker dispatch as a collapsed parent row nesting its tool calls; expand for raw CLI stream; two-level collapse mirrors the orchestrator/worker hierarchy

patterns.pattern: Live status line: animated verb, ticking elapsed time, streaming token count, interrupt hint
patterns.products: Claude Code (esc-to-interrupt), Cursor, Copilot, Zed (stop), Cline
patterns.why: a bare spinner lies; two moving numbers (time and tokens) make is-it-alive answerable at a glance
patterns.applicability: each worker chip shows a live heartbeat (last-output time/bytes) that degrades on stall (no output 45s); orchestrator status aggregates honest heartbeats; fixes the prior status-lying problem

patterns.pattern: Thinking as a separate dim collapsible block, off by default, never mixed into the answer
patterns.products: Claude Code (Ctrl+O), claude.ai (summarized), Cline, Cursor/Copilot status lines
patterns.why: demotes noisy reasoning so it is not mistaken for conclusions; status phrasing conveys intent
patterns.applicability: surface orchestrator dispatch rationale as a collapsed dim block, keep worker thinking in its sub-thread, use short status phrasing (Dispatching worker A, Merging results)

patterns.pattern: Edits as proposed red/green diffs with explicit Keep/Undo per-hunk, per-file, or all
patterns.products: GitHub Copilot, Zed (multibuffer), Cursor (Composer), Cline/Roo; Cursor no-diff auto-apply drew a revolt
patterns.why: trust needs a gate between agent-decided and files-changed; removing it reads as removing control
patterns.applicability: swarmx workers write in isolated worktrees; surface each as an aggregated diff reviewed before merge to main; gate moves from before-write to before-merge, with per-file accept to cherry-pick

patterns.pattern: Checkpoints plus one-click rewind (auto snapshots before each turn)
patterns.products: Claude Code (double-Esc), Cline/Roo, Copilot (Restore), Zed (Restore Checkpoint)
patterns.why: rewind makes exploration safe and aggressive autonomy acceptable
patterns.applicability: checkpoint per orchestrator turn and per worker dispatch; a bad worker rewinds in its own worktree without nuking siblings; separate worktrees make isolated rewind nearly free

patterns.pattern: Two steering modes: QUEUE (after the turn) vs STEER/INTERRUPT (in-flight at the next tool boundary)
patterns.products: Cursor (Alt+Enter queue, Cmd+Enter interrupt), Claude Code (Esc interrupts; Enter queues, a documented confusion), Zed
patterns.why: conflating them is the top forum complaint: a redirect gets silently queued and the agent goes wrong for minutes
patterns.applicability: offer queue-for-next-turn vs interrupt-now-halt-workers-replan; show queued messages as pending chips; route a steer to the orchestrator which can abort workers; never silently queue a course-correction

patterns.pattern: In-thread plan/todo list as a live checklist (pending, in-progress, completed)
patterns.products: Claude Code (TodoWrite/Task tools), Cursor (Plan Mode, Mermaid), Copilot (Plan agent, markdown plus JSON), Cline (Plan then Act)
patterns.why: lets the plan be corrected before execution, the biggest quality lever, and shows position and remaining work
patterns.applicability: orchestrator publishes an editable plan as a live checklist where each item is a worker dispatch; the human approves before workers spawn (Plan-to-Act gate); it is the contract and master progress view

patterns.pattern: Agent-centric (not file-centric) layout: parallel agents in isolated worktrees with aggregated diff review
patterns.products: Cursor 2.0 (up to 8 agents, fan-out), Cursor 2.2 (judging), Windsurf/Devin Desktop (Command Center, Kanban)
patterns.why: with many agents the file tree is the wrong axis; isolation stops clobbering, aggregated review picks the winner
patterns.applicability: swarmx home turf: primary view is worker lanes/chips (status, heartbeat, diff) in worktrees; review aggregated diffs and merge per-worker; optionally fan-out and judge; a Kanban beats a linear chat

antipatterns: auto-apply edits with no diff gate
silently queuing a redirect
spinner-only status
disabling interrupt
uncollapsed tool output
shared worker directory
shell edits bypassing review diff
stale plan lists
hidden or inlined reasoning
takeaways: honest two-signal status
two-level collapsible worker rows
merge-time accept/reject gate
distinct queue vs interrupt
orchestrator plan as live checklist
checkpoint per dispatch with rewind
agent-centric lane/Kanban shell
demote reasoning to dim blocks
capture all worker writes into review

rAutonomous.summary: Autonomous agents converge on one dominant layout for surviving minutes-long async work: a split screen where the LEFT is a scrolling conversation/narration and the RIGHT is a live "work surface" (Manus's "computer" pane, Devin's shell+editor+browser, Jules's diff pane, Operator's controlled browser, Bolt/v0/Lovable's live preview). The dead-time gap is filled by streaming low-latency signals — a live activity log of discrete tool calls (installing X, editing Y, running tests), plus a persistent plan/todo that visibly checks off (✓ done, → in-progress) — so the user sees motion within 1–2 seconds even though the whole task takes minutes. Trust comes from "showing the work" (every click, command, diff is visible and replayable) rather than a spinner; failure/stuck and needs-input states are first-class ("waiting_for_user", "take over the browser", "Attempt Fix"), and sessions persist/sleep so you can close the tab and get pinged in Slack when it's done. The recurring failure mode is the silent fix-loop (Bolt) where the agent cycles without telling the user it's stuck — the exact "status lies" problem swarmx already named. Everything maps cleanly onto swarmx's project-session model, 事实律/入流律, failure cards, inbox, and notifications.

patterns.pattern: Split screen: conversation on the left, a live 'work surface' on the right (the agent's computer/shell/editor/browser/preview), not interleaved into the chat
patterns.products: Manus ('Manus's Computer' side panel), Devin (shell+editor+browser panels), Google Jules (activity feed + full diff editor on right pane), OpenAI Operator/ChatGPT agent (chat left, controlled browser right), Bolt.new/v0/Lovable (chat left, live preview/code right). Described by Emerge Haus as the 'new dominant UI design': left ~50% chat history+input, right ~50% dynamic viewer of the agent's workspace.
patterns.why: During minutes-long work the chat thread alone becomes an unreadable firehose of tool spam OR goes silent. Splitting lets the conversation stay a clean human-readable narration while ALL the mechanical activity (browser tabs, file writes, shell output, diffs) streams into a dedicated pane the user can glance at or ignore. It also separates 'what the agent decided/said' (durable, scannable) from 'what the agent is doing right now' (high-volume, ephemeral). Split wins specifically when the agent produces a continuous artifact you'd want to watch evolve (a browser session, a building app, a growing diff); inline wins for short turn-based exchanges with little intermediate work.
patterns.applicability: swarmx already plans a session = 对话 / 终端 / 变更 three blocks — this is the same split, just with N agents. Make the right side a per-session 'work surface' tab group (terminal + diff/变更 + maybe a live preview), and keep the left as the conversation/narration. For multi-agent, the right pane can be a tabbed or stacked view (one work-surface per active 队员) so the conversation never gets buried under N agents' tool output.

patterns.pattern: Live activity log of discrete, named tool calls streamed within 1–2s ('Installing dependencies', 'Editing src/App.tsx', 'Running tests', 'Opened tripadvisor.com')
patterns.products: Google Jules ('live logs of what Jules is doing... think aloud, explaining why it changed each file'), Replit Agent (update messages whenever it installs a package / runs a shell command / creates a file), Devin (full replay timeline of every command, file diff, browser tab), Manus (every click and file write visible). Forum One / Telerik UX research: streaming tool-call events make a 15s wait feel responsive vs. a spinner where 'users don't know if the agent is working or crashed'.
patterns.why: A spinner or 'thinking…' gives zero evidence the agent is alive; a 15s silence 'feels like a minute.' A stream of concrete, human-readable actions proves liveness, communicates intent ('it's running my tests, good'), and lets the user catch a wrong turn early (going off-track). The key is latency: first signal must appear in ~1–2s, far before the task finishes.
patterns.applicability: This is swarmx's 入流律 (every state change must enter as a system card, server-persisted) made concrete. Each agent action (spawn, tool call, file edit, terminal command, merge) becomes a timestamped activity card in the session's 变更/activity stream. Critically: emit the FIRST card immediately on agent start so the user never stares at a green dot wondering if anything is happening — this directly fixes the 状态撒谎 root cause.

patterns.pattern: A persistent plan / todo list that visibly checks items off as the agent progresses (✓ done, → in-progress, ▢ pending)
patterns.products: Factory.ai Droid (plan tool marks the most recent step crossed off while marking the next as in-progress — 'an easy way for humans to track and supervise without combing the session'), Replit Agent (report_progress adds ✓ before completed, → before in-progress, ends with 'What's next?'), Devin (planner LLM expands goal into step plan, self-critiques), Cursor background agents (monitor progress via a workflow_state.md to-do list). Factory also pins this to the END of the context window so the agent itself stays on track.
patterns.why: A checking-off plan is the single highest-signal progress indicator: it shows where we are, what's left, and an implicit ETA — far more orienting than a raw log. It doubles as alignment (user can see the plan is correct before/while it runs) and as supervision (a human can audit a long session in seconds instead of reading every line). Bonus: it also keeps the agent on-rails, reducing drift.
patterns.applicability: swarmx's 计划 concept is exactly this. Render it as a live checklist at the top of the session (above the conversation/work surface) that updates in place as 队员 complete steps. For multi-agent, show which 队员 owns each step. This gives the session a visible 终点 — solving the diagnosed 'no destination / no承载物' problem — and lets the 队长 (and the user) supervise N parallel agents at a glance.

patterns.pattern: Approve-the-plan-before-run gate (Plan Mode): agent drafts an editable ordered plan, user clicks Start/Implement, then it executes
patterns.products: Replit Agent Plan Mode ('Start building' to approve; refine via conversation first; no code/data changes until approved), Bolt V2 ('Bolt lists every step', approve before 'Implement this plan'), Google Jules (approve the plan, then it codes and shows activity feed), Devin (planner self-critiques each step before execution).
patterns.why: For autonomous work that will run for minutes unattended, a wrong plan wastes the whole run. A cheap up-front approval converts an expensive late failure into a 10-second correction, and gives the user a contract they can hold the agent to. It also sets expectations for the dead-time wait ('I approved these 6 steps, now I wait').
patterns.applicability: swarmx can offer the 队长 a draft 计划 the human approves before dispatching 队员 — especially valuable because multi-agent runs are even more expensive to get wrong. Plan-approval is also a natural human-in-the-loop checkpoint that fits the project-session mental model: approve the plan = commit the session's scope.

patterns.pattern: Replayable, persistent activity timeline you can scrub after the fact (session replay / rollback to checkpoint)
patterns.products: Manus ('replay past sessions... roll back the timeline and observe each step'), Devin (vectorized memory + full replay timeline of every command/diff/browser tab), Replit Agent (checkpoints with a rollback button to revert to an earlier state), Factory.ai (favorite a session, pick it back up days later, fork a session).
patterns.why: Async by definition means the user is away while work happens. If the timeline isn't persisted, returning users are lost. Replay/checkpoints let them reconstruct what happened, audit decisions, and undo a bad stretch without redoing everything — turning a scary black box into something reviewable and reversible.
patterns.applicability: swarmx's 入流律 already mandates server-persisted state, and sessions archive when done. Lean into it: the session's activity stream IS the replay timeline; add checkpoint markers at meaningful boundaries (plan step done, merge, agent handoff) and let the user roll a session's 变更 back to a checkpoint. This makes closing the tab safe and makes parallel sessions auditable after the fact.

patterns.pattern: Async persistence + push notification on completion or when blocked ('come back to a finished PR')
patterns.products: Cursor background/cloud agents (run remotely, Slack notification + PR link when done; @Cursor list my agents; join live session from web), Devin (per-run 'Enable Slack notifications', DMs you on status updates; sessions sleep instead of ending and can be woken), Factory.ai (sessions sync across TUI/desktop/web/IDE/Slack — start in one, continue in another), Google Jules ('step away, it's asynchronous by design'), Cognition CLI ('Devin keeps working while you don't, so you come back to a finished PR').
patterns.why: The whole point of autonomous agents is that the user leaves. Without a notification, the user has to babysit (defeating the purpose) or forgets the task entirely. A push on done/blocked closes the loop, and cross-surface continuity means the work isn't trapped in one tab.
patterns.applicability: swarmx's redesign already includes 收件箱 + 通知. Wire notifications to two events: session finished (变更 ready for 评审/合并) and session blocked/needs-input. The 收件箱 becomes the cross-session aggregation point so the user can fire off N parallel sessions, walk away, and get pulled back only when a session needs them — this is the answer to the diagnosed '跨空间扫视零信号, 并行即失控'.

patterns.pattern: First-class 'needs your input' / 'take over' state that PAUSES and pulls the human in, instead of failing silently
patterns.products: OpenAI Operator/ChatGPT agent (pauses and prompts 'Take over browser' for logins/passwords; requests permission before consequential actions; on-screen narration; user can interrupt/take over/stop anytime), Devin (explicit session statuses 'waiting_for_user' and 'waiting_for_approval' in safe mode), Cursor (send follow-up instructions or 'take over' the agent's work mid-run).
patterns.why: Autonomous agents constantly hit walls only a human can clear (a login, an ambiguous choice, a risky action). The good designs make this a distinct, loud state that blocks and hands control over — not a silent stall. It also bounds risk: consequential actions get an approval gate.
patterns.applicability: swarmx needs an explicit 待输入/待批准 session status that surfaces as a failure-card-style inbox item and a notification, with the conversation as the natural handoff channel (the 队员 asks, the human answers inline). Reuse the 失败卡 原地翻转 pattern: a blocked session flips its card to a 'needs you' state in place rather than disappearing or pretending it's still 'running'.

patterns.pattern: Failure shown honestly and in place, with a bounded, named recovery action — and guard against the silent fix-loop
patterns.products: Bolt.new ('Attempt Fix' button; live preview surfaces build errors immediately) — but ALSO the cautionary tale: Bolt agents get 'stuck in a loop', cycling fixes because 'the agent never receives the exact failure message', and users are advised to 'stop debugging loops after 2-3 failed attempts'. Replit (rollback to checkpoint as the recovery). Devin guidance: if it 'seems stuck or going in circles', intervene and break the task down.
patterns.why: Errors are normal in autonomous work; hiding them or auto-retrying invisibly destroys trust and burns tokens. The right pattern is: detect, show the real error, offer one explicit recovery action, and CAP automatic retries so the agent escalates to the human instead of spinning forever. A silent fix-loop is the worst outcome — it looks like progress while making none.
patterns.applicability: This is swarmx's 状态撒谎 problem in autonomous form. Apply 事实律: a 队员 that hits an error must surface a failure card with the real error text, not keep a green dot. Cap auto-retries (e.g. 2-3) then flip the card to 'needs你 / 换引擎' — swarmx already planned a 换引擎按钮, which is the perfect bounded recovery action. Never let an agent loop silently; every retry must enter the stream (入流律).

antipatterns: Generic spinner / 'Thinking…' with no streamed detail — a 15s silence 'feels like a minute' and users can't tell if the agent is working or crashed (Forum One / Telerik UX research). swarmx's green-dot-that-only-means-process-exists is exactly this anti-pattern.

Silent fix-loops: the agent hits an error, auto-retries, breaks something else, and cycles — appearing busy while making no progress and burning tokens (Bolt.new's documented stuck-loop; guidance is to cap at 2-3 attempts). The user is never told it's stuck.

Dumping raw tool-call spam directly into the conversation thread — minutes of file writes / shell output drown the human-readable narration and make the session unscannable. This is why the split-pane exists.

Losing the timeline when the tab closes: async work whose activity log isn't persisted leaves returning users disoriented (Devin even notes that after long inactivity you may have to start a new session and re-share context — a degraded experience).

Status that describes process liveness instead of capability ('N agents online', 'engine ready') while the agent is actually rate-limited, logged out, or stalled — the core 状态撒谎 failure swarmx already diagnosed.

Blocked/needs-input states that don't loudly pull the human in — if the agent silently waits or stalls instead of raising a distinct 'waiting_for_user' state + notification, the user babysits or the task dies.
Expensive actions with no preview/cost signal — Bolt's 'Attempt Fix' silently re-sends the whole codebase (100k–200k tokens) per click; users can't see the cost of an action before triggering it.

Concept overload in the work surface — exposing internal engine jargon (worktree/blackboard/wake/spell) in the activity stream, the same over-budget-by-3x concept problem swarmx flagged; the activity log must speak in user verbs ('edited file', 'ran tests'), not kernel terms.
takeaways: Adopt the dominant split: conversation/narration on the left, a per-session live 'work surface' (终端 + 变更/diff + optional preview) on the right. swarmx's planned 对话/终端/变更 三块 IS this — formalize it and make the right side the home for all high-volume agent output so the conversation stays scannable.

Emit the FIRST activity card within 1-2 seconds of an agent starting and stream a discrete, plainly-named action per tool call. This is 入流律 made real and is the single most direct fix for the green-dot 状态撒谎 problem — proof of life beats a spinner.

Make the 计划 a live checklist that checks off in place (✓/→/▢), pinned above the session. It gives the session a visible 终点 (fixing 'no destination/承载物'), lets the 队长 and human supervise N agents at a glance, and keeps agents on-rails. Offer an approve-before-run gate for expensive multi-agent runs.

Treat blocked/failed/needs-input as first-class, loud states that flip the session card in place (失败卡 原地翻转) and fire a notification — never a silent stall or a persistent green dot. Apply 事实律: show the real error text. Cap auto-retries at 2-3 then escalate to the human with a bounded recovery action (swarmx's 换引擎按钮 fits perfectly).

Make async truly async: persist the activity timeline (it's the replay), add checkpoints you can roll back to, and push a notification on done-or-blocked into the 收件箱. This lets the user fire N parallel sessions, close the tab, and be pulled back only when needed — the answer to '并行即失控'.

Keep the activity log in user-facing verbs, not engine internals — 'edited App.tsx / ran tests / opened PR', never worktree/blackboard/wake/spell — consistent with the diagnosed concept-overload problem.

rMultiAgent.summary: Across the field, the orchestrator-worker relationship is made legible through one of three primary metaphors, and the choice is driven by how parallel the work is and whether output is text or artifacts. (1) Build/debug tools (LangGraph Studio, AutoGen Studio, CrewAI traces, OpenAI Agents traces, ChatDev ChatChain) use a graph/DAG or timeline view for structure plus a span/trace tree for execution — great for inspection, but they are developer dashboards, not a live "watch the team work" surface. (2) Conversational research systems (Anthropic's multi-agent research, Claude Code sub-agents) keep ONE human-facing stream and deliberately hide inter-agent chatter: subagents run in isolated context windows and only condensed findings/final messages bubble up, with a collapsible roster/agent-view for drill-in. (3) Parallel coding-agent managers (Vibe Kanban, Conductor, Crystal/Nimbalyst, cmux, Agentastic) abandon a single stream entirely for a kanban/sidebar-of-workspaces, where each agent owns an isolated lane (git worktree) and the key UX primitive is a "needs attention" badge that pulls the human in only at decision points. The consistent winning pattern is altitude control: a scannable summary (status per agent) as the default, verbose per-agent logs one click away, and an explicit, event-shaped representation of delegation and handoff rather than a firehose of raw chatter. swarmx's current design (single Telegram stream + roster + separate DAG tab) is a reasonable hybrid but under-uses status-on-the-roster and lacks an event grammar for delegation/handoff and an attention-routing signal.

patterns.pattern: Two-level altitude: a scannable roster/dashboard of agents (status + last action) as the default view, with each agent's full verbose log exactly one click away
patterns.products: Claude Code Agent View, Vibe Kanban, Conductor, Agentastic, AutoGen Studio playground
patterns.why: Solves the core tension of parallelism: humans need to know WHICH agent needs them without reading every agent's output. The summary panel shows all agents with current status + last action ('Reading src/Header.tsx', 'Running npm test') for quick scanning; clicking one reveals its full output log. This prevents the cognitive overload of N simultaneous terminal windows while keeping full transparency for debugging.
patterns.applicability: swarmx already has the right-side member roster — but today it appears to be identity-only. Promote it to a live status board: each ephemeral worker row shows a state pill (spawning/working/blocked/done/failed) + a one-line 'current action'. The roster becomes the summary level; tapping a worker filters the single stream to just that agent's messages (the drill-in). This is the cheapest high-leverage change.

patterns.pattern: One human-facing stream, with inter-agent coordination hidden by default — only condensed findings / the orchestrator's synthesized message are promoted to the human
patterns.products: Anthropic multi-agent research system, Claude Code sub-agents
patterns.why: Subagents run in their own isolated context windows and return CONDENSED findings (often via shared memory, not chat-style returns); the lead agent reconciles them into a single final answer with citations. In Claude Code, the parent receives the subagent's final message verbatim as the tool result but typically summarizes it — verbose work stays in the subagent's context. This is the explicit antidote to the firehose: raw worker-to-worker chatter is internal coordination, NOT broadcast to the human.
patterns.applicability: Directly maps to swarmx's 1 human + 1 orchestrator + N ephemeral workers. Default the single stream to show ONLY: the human's messages, the orchestrator's plan/synthesis, and worker COMPLETIONS (condensed). Treat raw worker token-streams and worker-to-orchestrator handoffs as collapsed/internal by default, expandable on demand. The orchestrator should be the one 'voice' that speaks to the human; workers speak through it unless drilled into.

patterns.pattern: Per-agent isolated lanes backed by real isolation (git worktree / separate context window), surfaced as kanban columns or a workspace sidebar instead of a shared transcript
patterns.products: Vibe Kanban (To Do→In Progress→Review→Done), Conductor (workspace sidebar + per-workspace chat+diff), Crystal/Nimbalyst, cmux
patterns.why: When agents do heavyweight parallel work that produces ARTIFACTS (code, diffs, PRs) rather than chat, a single stream is the wrong container — you can't interleave 5 agents' file edits readably. Each workspace gets a dedicated branch/worktree so agents work in clean isolation 'preventing the chaos of multiple AIs conflicting'. The board/sidebar is the orchestration surface; each lane is a self-contained world you enter to review.
patterns.applicability: swarmx's separate DAG tab is the structural cousin of this. If swarmx workers ever produce durable artifacts (files, sub-results), consider letting a worker lane 'pop out' into its own focused view (its own mini-thread + any artifacts), reachable from the roster or DAG node. Keep the Telegram stream for the conversational/coordination layer, but give artifact-heavy workers a dedicated drill-in surface rather than dumping everything inline.

patterns.pattern: 'Needs attention' as a first-class routing signal: an orange/badge indicator + native notification pulls the human to exactly the agent that is blocked or done, instead of making them poll
patterns.products: Agentastic (orange indicator when waiting for input), AgentsRoom (per-project unread-count badge + macOS notification on completion), Vibe Kanban ('Needs Attention' state), Warp, SlayZone attention panel
patterns.why: With parallel agents the human's scarce resource is attention, not screen space. A state machine detects idle/working/attention states; agents needing human input surface in a notification panel, and completion fires a native notification you click to jump straight to that agent. This inverts the model from 'human watches everything' to 'system summons human when it matters'.
patterns.applicability: swarmx should give each worker an explicit BLOCKED/NEEDS-INPUT state distinct from WORKING, and badge it on the roster + (optionally) push a notification. Given swarmx's Telegram-style framing, an unread/attention badge per worker row and a top-level 'N workers need you' counter would let a human run many ephemeral workers without babysitting the stream. This pairs naturally with the honesty work already shipped (don't lie about status) — 'needs attention' must be truthful.

patterns.pattern: Delegation and handoff rendered as a discrete, typed EVENT in the timeline (not just a message), with its own visual treatment
patterns.products: OpenAI Agents SDK traces (handoff spans), Magentic-One (orchestrator emits 'Initial plan created', 'Replanned', 'Progress ledger updated' events; next-speaker + instruction), CrewAI traces, OpenAI Swarm ('transferred to X' like a phone transfer)
patterns.why: Audit and legibility depend on a stable grammar of events: the trace preview shows 'whether any handoffs occurred', you can 'audit every hand-off and understand how the workflow evolved turn by turn'. Magentic surfaces planning/progress as named milestone events with the explicit next speaker and the instruction sent to them. Making delegation an event (vs an indistinguishable chat line) lets the UI badge it, collapse around it, and show who-handed-what-to-whom.
patterns.applicability: In swarmx's single stream, render 'orchestrator → spawned worker_3 to do X' and 'worker_3 → returned result to orchestrator' as distinct system/event chips (different shape/color from chat bubbles), each anchored to the agent's color. These event chips become the skeleton the human reads when skimming; the chatty content hangs off them collapsibly. This also gives the DAG tab and the stream a shared vocabulary (a handoff event = an edge in the DAG).

patterns.pattern: Stable per-agent identity: consistent color + avatar + role label reused everywhere the agent appears (stream attribution, roster row, DAG node)
patterns.products: ChatDev (named roles CEO/CTO/Programmer/Tester/Reviewer), AutoGen Studio, CrewAI (role-playing agents), Claude Code (named subagents with status line)
patterns.why: With many agents, attribution is the load-bearing affordance. Role-named agents ('Tester', 'Reviewer') plus a fixed color let the human parse @-attributed lines in a shared stream and recognize the same actor in the roster and the graph. Consistency across surfaces is what makes a single stream survivable at N>3 agents.
patterns.applicability: swarmx's ephemeral workers need stable, glanceable identity for their (short) lifetime: assign each spawned worker a color + a role-derived label (not just 'worker_7') and reuse that exact color/label as the message author chip in the stream, the roster row, and the DAG node. Because workers are ephemeral, lean on role/task-derived names ('search-pricing', 'verify-claims') over generic IDs so the human reads intent, not handles.

patterns.pattern: Graph/DAG view as the structural map kept SEPARATE from the live execution feed — structure answers 'how is this wired', the feed answers 'what's happening now'
patterns.products: LangGraph Studio (StateGraph flowchart, step-through/time-travel debug), ChatDev ChatChain Visualizer, AutoGen Studio canvas, CrewAI timeline
patterns.why: These tools deliberately split the static topology (nodes/edges, who-can-talk-to-whom) from the dynamic trace (spans, timeline of what actually ran). The graph is for understanding and editing structure; the trace/timeline is for watching and debugging a run. Conflating them produces a graph that's either too noisy to read or a feed with no sense of place.
patterns.applicability: Validates swarmx's choice to keep the DAG in a separate tab. Improve the linkage: let the live stream and the DAG cross-highlight (clicking a DAG node filters the stream to that agent; an active handoff event in the stream pulses the corresponding DAG edge). The DAG should also animate liveness — color nodes by current state (idle/working/done/failed) so the separate tab isn't purely static topology but a glanceable 'team heartbeat'.

antipatterns: Streaming every agent's raw token output into one shared transcript (the 'firehose'): with N parallel agents the interleaved stream becomes unreadable, and Claude Code literally has filed bugs about terminal UI corruption when running ~18 parallel Task subagents. Inter-agent chatter must be internal coordination, not broadcast.

A status line / roster that doesn't actually report concurrent agent state: Claude Code's own users filed feature requests because the CLI status line shows context/token usage but has 'no equivalent indicator for concurrent subagent activity' — identity without live status forces the human to guess or poll.

Lying about status (showing 'working' for a stalled/blocked/finished agent). Magentic-One's whole progress-ledger + stall-counter design exists to detect loops/no-progress; a UI that can't distinguish working vs blocked vs needs-input vs done erodes trust. (swarmx's prior honesty work targets exactly this.)

Making the human poll N tabs/windows to discover who needs them — the reason 'needs attention' badges + native completion notifications exist. Forcing manual round-robin across agents doesn't scale past ~3 agents.
Generic opaque agent IDs ('worker_7', 'agent-3') with no role/color identity: kills attribution in a shared stream and makes the roster and DAG unreadable. Names should carry intent.
Treating delegation/handoff as an ordinary chat line indistinguishable from content — it can't be collapsed around, badged, or mapped to a DAG edge, so the human loses the skeleton of who-did-what.
A DAG/graph that is purely static topology with no liveness — it shows wiring but not state, so the human still has to go elsewhere to learn what's happening, defeating the point of a separate view.
Dumping a subagent's full verbose log into the parent conversation by default instead of a condensed result — bloats context and buries the synthesized answer the human actually wants.
takeaways: Promote swarmx's right-side roster from identity-only to a live status board: each worker row = color/role label + state pill (spawning/working/blocked/done/failed) + one-line current action. This single change turns the roster into the 'summary altitude' that every parallel-agent tool relies on.

Default the single stream to the orchestrator's voice + worker COMPLETIONS only; collapse raw worker streams and worker→orchestrator handoffs as internal-by-default, expandable on tap. The orchestrator synthesizes; workers speak through it unless drilled into. (Mirrors Anthropic research system + Claude Code sub-agents.)

Give workers an explicit BLOCKED / NEEDS-INPUT state distinct from WORKING, badge it on the roster, add a top-level 'N workers need you' counter, and consider a push notification on completion/block. Invert from 'human watches' to 'system summons'. Keep it truthful (ties to your honesty work).

Introduce an event grammar: render delegation ('orchestrator spawned worker_3 for X') and return ('worker_3 returned result') as distinct system/event chips in the stream, color-anchored to the agent. These chips are the readable skeleton; chatty content hangs off them collapsibly — and each chip maps 1:1 to a DAG edge.

Tapping a worker (in roster, stream chip, or DAG node) should filter the stream to that agent — make 'drill into one agent' a first-class, reversible focus mode rather than a separate screen. Cross-highlight stream and DAG so the two views share state.

Make the DAG tab live, not static: color nodes by current state and pulse edges on active handoffs, so the separate structural view doubles as a glanceable team heartbeat. Keep structure (DAG) and execution (stream) separate but cross-linked — validated by LangGraph Studio / AutoGen splitting canvas from trace.

Use role/task-derived names + stable colors for ephemeral workers ('search-pricing', 'verify-claims') instead of opaque IDs, and reuse the exact identity across stream/roster/DAG so attribution survives at N>3 agents.

Decide per-output-type where work lives: conversational coordination stays in the Telegram stream; if a worker ever produces durable artifacts, let it pop out into a focused per-worker lane (à la Vibe Kanban/Conductor worktree workspaces) reachable from its roster row or DAG node.
sources: https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/27916 (FEATURE: display active subagent count in CLI status line)
https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/48246 (FEATURE: show agent/subagent task progress in terminal UI)
https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/21690 (BUG: terminal UI corruption with 18 parallel Task subagents)

rPrinciples.summary: Foundational chat/AI UX consensus for an agent chat. Detail in patterns.

patterns.pattern: Honest capability/reliability up front, no fake online: status words state only server-confirmed facts; a green dot must mean can-do-work not process-exists. Disclaimers near the composer not a footer.
patterns.products: Microsoft HAX G1/G2; NN/g explainable-AI; Google PAIR; Shape of AI Caveat/Disclosure/Footprints
patterns.why: Overtrust collapses when a silently-failing system is exposed; the first minute decides trust. Calibrated expectations stop users blaming themselves for invisible failures.
patterns.applicability: swarmx Phase-1 truth-law already does this. Extend the status chip to real readiness (logged-in, not rate-limited, responsive); keep a recovery path on every error (audited bug was a one-way latch).

patterns.pattern: Immediate, specific, streaming feedback for slow work: respond under ~400ms, token-stream, narrate steps (reading X, editing Y) not an indeterminate spinner; prefer skeletons.
patterns.products: Doherty 400ms; Nielsen 0.1/1/10s; skeleton research ~20% faster; Vercel AI SDK streaming; Shape of AI Stream-of-Thought
patterns.why: Agent turns exceed the 10s attention threshold, so latency must be narrated; streaming keeps users doing not waiting.
patterns.applicability: Make swarmx's typing indicator specific (tool/file/step names); replace fake progress / no-messages with the startup checklist; the 90s watchdog is the backstop.

patterns.pattern: Separate the human conversation from the agent activity/event timeline: thread = goals; a distinct activity panel shows tool calls, decisions, blocked/next, collapsible.
patterns.products: Hatchworks chat-first-fails; Honeycomb agent timeline; AG-UI; ServiceNow
patterns.why: Many agent actions between user messages make an interleaved thread unreadable and hide work structure; users distrust what they cannot see.
patterns.applicability: swarmx session = three blocks (conversation, terminal, changes): conversation = goal thread, per-member activity timeline, aggregate inbox for cross-space scan.

patterns.pattern: Show what the agent DID with progressive disclosure: action receipts, citations beside claims, collapsible show-work. Do NOT present raw chain-of-thought as proof of correctness.
patterns.products: Shape of AI Citations/Footprints/Action-plan; Hatchworks Action-Receipts/Evidence-Panel; NN/g (avoid CoT as unfaithful); HAX G11/G4
patterns.why: Trust comes from verifiable artifacts (diff, path, source) more than narrated reasoning, which can be unfaithful.
patterns.applicability: swarmx changes block = receipt surface: each change a receipt (files, branch, rollback, worker); present diff and test result, never private reasoning, as the guarantee.

patterns.pattern: Human-in-the-loop steering as a first-class surface: real stop/pause/resume, approval gates before irreversible acts, message queueing while generating.
patterns.products: Vercel AI SDK 6 needsApproval + stop; AG-UI INTERRUPT; Hatchworks gates/autonomy levels; Shape of AI Controls/Verification; HAX G7/G8/G9/G17/G16
patterns.why: Agents that cannot be stopped or corrected feel unsafe; interrupt/approve/queue keep the human in control and prevent irreversible mistakes.
patterns.applicability: swarmx runs real code-acting CLIs: per-member Stop, approval gate before merge/destructive git, per-session autonomy level, queue follow-ups; merge gate = checkpoint.

patterns.pattern: Reduce concept count and name things once: cap at ~5 plus/minus 2, avoid one-thing-three-names, keep jargon out of the UI.
patterns.products: Microsoft G5; NN/g match-system-to-real-world; Shape of AI Name/Iconography
patterns.why: Each extra concept and synonym taxes recognition and trust; a small vocabulary makes the UI learnable in the first session.
patterns.applicability: swarmx had ~9 concepts and one-thing-three-names (orchestrator=scheduler=choreographer) plus kernel jargon (worktree/blackboard/wake/spell); the 5-concept model (project, session, member, plan, changes) is right, one canonical label each.

patterns.pattern: Composer: multiline default with documented send model (Enter vs Shift+Enter), slash and at under one popover, attachments/connectors, prompt assistance, per-session draft persistence on mount.
patterns.products: assistant-ui / Chat SDK; Codex/Copilot/GitLab Duo; Shape of AI Open-input/Suggestions/Templates/Prompt-enhancer; getstream
patterns.why: The composer is where prompt quality (NN/g) is won; discoverable slash/at fix blank-canvas paralysis; draft persistence prevents losing a long prompt.
patterns.applicability: swarmx: multiline + documented Enter/Shift+Enter; slash for session actions, at to target member/file; per-session draft persistence; capability-revealing starter prompts.

patterns.pattern: Designed empty and error states. Empty: capability-revealing greeting + example prompts. Error: model the lifecycle, plain-language cause, reassure work is saved, 2-3 recovery options; never dead-end.
patterns.products: Google PAIR Errors+Graceful-Failure; Gemini empty-state cards; NN/g; uxpatterns.dev; Shape of AI Suggestions/Templates; HAX G9/G15
patterns.why: The empty state is the cheapest onboarding; the error state is where trust is most fragile; recoverable failures with saved work continue engagement.
patterns.applicability: swarmx failure card embodies this; generalize so rate-limit/crash/timeout each get a tailored card; finish the switch-engine button (CLI override on RunSpellRequest); replace no-messages with checklist/starter prompts.

antipatterns: fake-online status; narration-less spinners; tool-calls dumped into chat; chain-of-thought sold as correctness; one-way error latches; anthropomorphic overtrust; concept/jargon overload; buried disclaimers; no Stop/approval gate; lost drafts; blank empty + dead-end errors; HITL/receipts/audit as afterthoughts
takeaways: honesty first; specific streaming + startup checklist; split conversation from a per-member activity timeline; receipts/citations not chain-of-thought; HITL Stop/approval/autonomy/queue; finish composer (multiline, slash+at, draft persistence); 5-concept named-once vocabulary; full error lifecycle with always-present recovery
